content.text:
one concern I have about our current implementation is the markdown parser. Obsidian Flavored Markdown is a combination of CommonMark with GFM and other custom extensions: https://obsidian.md/help/obsidian-flavored-markdown
having a custom parser could be good enough for validating an MVP, but long-term it might become a liability and an unnecessary source of complexity. 
unfortunately, it looks like Obsidian doesn't provide an API for plugins to use its own parser. but I've seen claims like

> Obsidian uses remark-parse 8 or earlier with commonmark option enabled, as core parser.
[https://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-markdown-parser/55922/5](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-markdown-parser/55922/5?u=laughedelic)

and some plugins using mdast with extensions: https://forum.obsidian.md/t/access-parsed-markdown/25398

I want you to do more research on this and weigh pros and cons of using one of those solutions vs. having our own parser.

1	# Obsidian API Feasibility
2	
3	The core question: **can a true-outliner experience be built with public plugin APIs only —
4	no private APIs, no monkey-patching, a clean automated-review scorecard?**
5	
6	Short answer: **yes for the editor-centric architecture; no (today) for a Kanban-style
7	custom-view takeover of `.md` files.** Details below.
8	
9	## The constraint set ("perfect scorecard")
10	
11	As of 2026 Obsidian runs a [Community directory with automated review](https://obsidian.md/blog/future-of-plugins/):
12	every plugin *version* is scanned for security and code quality, and each plugin page shows a
13	**safety scorecard** (with planned disclosure of network/filesystem/clipboard access). Manual
14	review continues for popular plugins. Compliance is checkable locally via the official
15	[`obsidianmd/eslint-plugin`](https://github.com/obsidianmd/eslint-plugin).
16	
17	Relevant hard rules from the [Developer policies](https://docs.obsidian.md/Developer+policies)
18	and [Plugin guidelines](https://docs.obsidian.md/Plugins/Releasing/Plugin+guidelines):
19	
20	- No telemetry, no ads, no self-updating, no code obfuscation.
21	- Security: no `innerHTML`/`outerHTML` with user data — build DOM via `createEl()` etc.
22	- Vault API over Adapter; `Editor` API for active-note edits; `Vault.process()` for background
23	  edits; `processFrontMatter()` for YAML; `normalizePath()`; no `workspace.activeLeaf` access;
24	  register everything for auto-cleanup; no default hotkeys; sentence case UI; no Node/Electron
25	  APIs if mobile is supported.
26	
27	Note: the policies do not *explicitly* ban private-API use — but monkey-patching internals is
28	exactly what the code-quality scan and reviewers look for, it's the community's #1 breakage
29	source, and the project's own bar ("no private APIs or hacks") rules it out regardless.
30	
31	## What Obsidian already gives us (public, documented)
32	
33	This list is the pleasant surprise of the research — the platform provides more outliner
34	primitives than any of the reference apps had to start from:
35	
36	1. **The entire CodeMirror 6 extension surface is official API.**
37	   `Plugin.registerEditorExtension()` accepts arbitrary CM6 extensions: `StateField`,
38	   `ViewPlugin`, `keymap` (with `Prec.highest`), **`transactionFilter` / `transactionExtender`**
39	   (veto or rewrite any document/selection change → this is the enforcement mechanism),
40	   `Decoration.replace/widget/line` (hide syntax, draw node chrome, block widgets),
41	   `atomicRanges` (make bullet markers cursor-atomic), fold service, drop handlers.
42	   obsidian-outliner, obsidian-zoom and pro-outliner jointly prove Enter/Tab grammar, stick-to-
43	   content cursors, subtree hiding (zoom), selection limiting, selection expansion, drag-and-
44	   drop, mirrors, and breadcrumb headers all work through this surface.
45	2. **Core parses list structure for the whole vault already.** `CachedMetadata.listItems` gives
46	   every list item's span and **`parent` pointer** (plus task state) for every markdown file,
47	   kept fresh by the metadata cache — a vault-wide outline index for free. Combined with
48	   `resolvedLinks` (also public), this is everything needed for structured backlinks *without
49	   touching private search internals*.
50	3. **Block identity is native.** `^block-id` IDs, `[[note#^id]]` links, `![[note#^id]]` embeds
51	   are core Obsidian markdown — indexed (`CachedMetadata.blocks`), rendered, hover-previewed
52	   and search-integrated by core. A true outliner can adopt them as its node-reference syntax
53	   and inherit the whole ecosystem's compatibility, instead of inventing `id::` pollution
54	   (Logseq's mistake) or `((refs))`.
55	4. **Folding, Live Preview, `foldInfo`** — native list folding in the editor;
56	   `MarkdownView`/`editor:toggle-fold`; Live Preview already demonstrates syntax-hiding UX.
57	5. **Workspace/view APIs** — `registerView` for our own panes (structured backlinks, breadcrumb
58	   bar via `addStatusBarItem`/view header, etc.), `ItemView`, `MarkdownRenderer.render` for
59	   rendering node content in our panes.
60	6. **Events + `Vault.process`** for cross-file operations (mirror sync, refactors) done safely.
61	
62	## Architecture options
63	
64	### Option A — Editor-centric: CM6 extensions inside the standard markdown view ("recommended")
65	
66	The markdown view stays; the plugin makes it *behave* like an outliner for outline documents:
67	
68	- **Enforcement**: a `transactionFilter` normalizes every transaction — deletions crossing node
69	  boundaries become structural deletions; pastes are re-indented to valid depth; a line can
70	  never lose its bullet while in outline mode; selection changes escalate to node selection when
71	  crossing boundaries (pro-outliner already ships a v1 of this).
72	- **Grammar**: high-precedence keymaps for Enter/Shift+Enter/Tab/moves (proven).
73	- **Node UX**: decorations for bullets/handles/fold chevrons; block-select highlight via line
74	  decorations; zoom via replace-decorations + breadcrumbs (proven by obsidian-zoom).
75	- **Scope control**: extensions are registered globally but no-op unless the file is an outline
76	  document (frontmatter flag / folder / global mode — open question), checked via metadataCache.
77	
78	*Pros*: 100% public API; perfect-scorecard compatible; mobile works; degrades gracefully (files
79	are plain markdown lists everywhere else); interops with the whole ecosystem (backlinks, graph,
80	search, sync, other plugins). *Cons*: we inherit markdown-view constraints — the DOM is CM6's
81	line-based DOM, so some pixel-perfect block UI (e.g., Notion-style hover menus per block) takes
82	more creativity; "hard" enforcement is a filter over a text buffer, which demands a rigorous,
83	well-tested invariant layer (this is exactly the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from
84	the TUI prototype).
85	
86	### Option B — Custom view: parse md → own block editor (Kanban/Excalidraw pattern)
87	
88	A `TextFileView` renders the outline as a fully custom (e.g., per-node contenteditable/CM)
89	block UI; the file stays markdown on disk.
90	
91	The blocker: **there is no public mechanism to open `.md` files in a custom view.**
92	Kanban does it by monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState` (confirmed in source: intercepts
93	`state.type === 'markdown'`, checks for its frontmatter key, rewrites to its view type) — a
94	private-API hack, fragile, and contrary to this project's bar. A
95	[2025 forum thread](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/custom-viewer-ui-for-certain-markdown-files/108104)
96	asking for an official alternative got no answer from the team. Alternatives all lose something
97	essential: a custom extension (`.outline`) forfeits metadataCache/links/graph/mobile-editor
98	interop; "open via command" per file is UX friction and re-breaks on every workspace restore.
99	
100	*Verdict*: keep as a possible far-future layer (or revisit if Obsidian ships official
101	view-association APIs); not a foundation compatible with today's goals.
102	
103	### Option C — Hybrid (A + owned side surfaces)
104	
105	Option A for editing, plus our own `registerView` panes where we fully control rendering:
106	**structured backlinks pane** (tree context from `listItems`, editable via `Vault.process` or
107	embedded editors), zoom breadcrumb header, optional outline-wide search/filter view. All public.
108	This captures most of what Option B wanted anyway, without the takeover problem.
109	
110	## Feasibility scorecard per target feature
111	
112	| Target behavior | Public-API path | Confidence |
113	| --- | --- | --- |
114	| Enter/Tab/move/merge grammar | CM6 keymaps (proven in prod) | High |
115	| Structure enforcement | `transactionFilter` invariant layer | High (novel engineering, proven primitives) |
116	| Node selection model | transactionFilter + decorations (pro-outliner v1 exists) | High |
117	| Fold + persistence | native folds; storage decision open (plugin data vs in-file) | High |
118	| Zoom + breadcrumbs | replace-decorations (proven: obsidian-zoom) | High |
119	| Drag & drop | CM6 drop + widgets (proven: obsidian-outliner) | High |
120	| Multiline nodes | md continuation lines + grammar keymaps | Medium-high (careful spec needed) |
121	| Block refs/embeds | native `^id` / `![[#^id]]` | High (core does the heavy lifting) |
122	| Mirrors (synced blocks) | native embeds first; true editable mirrors later | Medium |
123	| Structured backlinks pane (read) | own view + `resolvedLinks` + `listItems` | High |
124	| Editable backlinks | + `Vault.process`/embedded editor per block | Medium |
125	| Mobile | all of the above is CM6/DOM | High (test discipline) |
126	| Perfect scorecard | eslint-plugin from day 1; no `any`-casts into internals | High under Option A |
127	
128	Two honesty notes: (a) `getBacklinksForFile` is **not** public — the public path is
129	`resolvedLinks` + own reverse index (the [backlink-cache](https://github.com/mnaoumov/obsidian-backlink-cache)
130	plugin shows the pattern); (b) even obsidian-outliner reaches for `(editor as any).cm` — we can
131	avoid it almost everywhere by living inside registered extensions (a `ViewPlugin` receives its
132	`EditorView` directly), which should be an explicit engineering rule.
133	
134	## Addendum: the universal isomorphic outline view (post-alignment)
135	
136	The 2026-07-12 alignment (see [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md), Q2) widened the
137	document model from "lists" to the **full markdown block tree** — headings, paragraphs, code
138	fences, quotes, tables *and* list items are all nodes; the outline is a toggleable editor UI
139	over any note, with a lossless (isomorphic) round-trip. Feasibility notes for that vision:
140	
141	- **The parse already exists.** `CachedMetadata.sections` (typed top-level blocks with
142	  positions), `headings` (text + level + position) and `listItems` (span + parent pointer)
143	  together describe exactly this block tree for every file in the vault, maintained by core.
144	  The editor side can parse incrementally from the CM6 document (and/or the bundled
145	  `@codemirror/language` syntax tree) for keystroke-latency accuracy; the cache serves
146	  cross-file features.
147	- **The CM6 architecture is unchanged.** Enforcement (`transactionFilter`), grammar (keymaps),
148	  node chrome (decorations), zoom (replace-decorations) apply to heading/paragraph nodes the
149	  same way they apply to list items. Obsidian's own fold system already treats *both* headings
150	  and list indents as foldable trees — precedent inside the core editor itself.
151	- **The new hard problem is the mapping algebra, not the APIs.** Headings encode depth by
152	  level, lists by indentation, paragraphs by adjacency — three different depth encodings in one
153	  tree. Operations must be closed over "trees expressible as natural markdown" (e.g.
154	  paragraph-under-paragraph has no markdown form → the op must convert, reject, or remap;
155	  h6 bounds heading depth). This is a spec/design problem — precisely what the OpenSpec
156	  explore/design phase is for — not an API gap.
157	- **Prior art**: org-mode is the closest living system (heading tree + plain lists with
158	  subtree promote/demote/move); no Obsidian plugin attempts the unified mapping. Obsidian's
159	  own "fold anything" + `sections` cache suggest the platform was shaped by adjacent thinking.
160	
161	## Bottom line
162	
163	The desired experience is achievable **within the rules** via Option A/C. The differentiating
164	work is not exotic API access — it's (1) a rigorously specified and tested **invariant/
165	enforcement layer** over CM6 transactions, (2) a coherent **node-selection model**, and (3) a
166	**structured backlinks pane** built on the vault-wide list index. The reference precedents
167	(outliner, zoom, pro-outliner, influx) collectively de-risk nearly every primitive; no existing
168	plugin has assembled them into one enforced, coherent product.
169

1	# Open Questions & Decisions
2	
3	Alignment log for pre-planning decisions. ✅ = decided, ❓ = open.
4	Three alignment rounds on 2026-07-12; all pre-planning questions are now decided except Q10
5	(explicitly post-v1).
6	
7	## Q1. Architecture path ✅ DECIDED: editor-centric (Option A/C)
8	
9	CM6 extensions in the standard markdown view + our own side panes. 100% public API.
10	See [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md).
11	
12	## Q2. Scope of "outline mode" ✅ DECIDED: universal isomorphic outline view
13	
14	Neither per-file opt-in nor vault-wide enforcement. The owner's framing (near-verbatim):
15	
16	> The outliner experience is all about the **editor UI, not the content under it**. Any "flat"
17	> markdown note actually has structure: a hierarchy implied by headings of different levels,
18	> each paragraph is a separate node, lists are natural outliner subtrees. Any existing markdown
19	> note can be transformed into an outline by mapping that hierarchical structure onto the tree,
20	> providing the same editing experience as for a nested bullet-list outline. A UI toggle turns
21	> any note into an outline (no change for list-only content), and back. **This has to be an
22	> isomorphic transformation.**
23	
24	Implications:
25	
26	- The document model is the **full markdown block tree**: heading nodes (level = depth
27	  anchor), block nodes (paragraph, code fence, quote, table, …), and list-item nodes —
28	  not just lists. Obsidian's `CachedMetadata` (`sections` + `headings` + `listItems`) already
29	  parses exactly this tree — see the feasibility addendum.
30	- The toggle is a **view/UI mode change, never a content rewrite**. For a pure-list note the
31	  outline view is visually identical to today's list editing.
32	- **Isomorphism = lossless round-trip**: md → tree → md must be identity; every structural
33	  operation in outline mode must produce a tree that maps back to valid, natural markdown.
34	- "Both without compromises": flat-markdown notes keep full outliner features *through the
35	  mapping*, instead of losing them (obsidian-outliner's model) or being forced into bullets
36	  (Logseq's model).
37	
38	### Q2 follow-ups (the mapping algebra) ✅ DECIDED (2026-07-12, second alignment)
39	
40	**The unifying principle** (governs all structural ops):
41	
42	> Every structural op either writes the **minimal markdown encoding of the new tree**, or —
43	> when no encoding exists — is **rejected with gentle feedback**. Never hidden state, never
44	> lossy conversion.
45	
46	**The two-regime algebra** (third alignment refinement):
47	
48	```
49	HEADINGS         Tab/S-Tab = level ± 1 (org-mode promote/demote), whole
50	                 subtree shifts; the tree re-derives from levels; reject
51	                 only at the h1/h6 bounds.
52	EVERYTHING ELSE  Tab/S-Tab = reparent (child-of-previous-sibling /
53	                 brother→uncle); encoding recomputed from the new context.
54	ALWAYS           minimal encoding or reject; no hidden state.
55	```
56	
57	1. **Paragraph under paragraph** ✅ *provisional*: a list following a paragraph is that
58	   paragraph's **children** in outline mode. So indenting paragraph B under paragraph A turns
59	   B into a list item after A; A stays an intact paragraph. Top-level paragraphs are never
60	   auto-converted — a flat document just becomes a long flat list of nodes when toggled.
61	   *Marked provisional*: revisit after the first prototype; may become configurable.
62	   *Alternatives considered and rejected*: (a) lists attach only to headings, never
63	   paragraphs — makes paragraph-with-children inexpressible, killing the indent-under-
64	   paragraph op entirely; (b) sentinel syntax (e.g. a paragraph ending in `:` claims the
65	   following list) — magic content-sniffing, fails isomorphism. Adjacency-as-parenthood is
66	   the only lossless way to give paragraphs children; the heading/paragraph asymmetry is
67	   markdown's (headings *scope* what follows; paragraphs merely *precede* it).
68	2. **Heading nodes** ✅: Tab/Shift+Tab = **level ± 1** (org-mode promote/demote semantics),
69	   the whole subtree's headings shift with it (marker-only edits; `[[note#Heading]]` anchors
70	   are text-based and survive). Rejected only at the bounds (no h0, no h7). **Skipped levels**
71	   (h1 → h3) are preserved; tree depth = tree position, not raw level; outdenting a
72	   skip-leveled heading first normalizes the level (### → ## under an h1: level changes,
73	   hierarchy doesn't), then the next outdent changes hierarchy — and symmetrically, Tab on a
74	   heading may create a skip (a "styling-only" edit where the node's tree position is
75	   unchanged and only the visible level marker deepens). Accepted consequence, same as org.
76	3. **Context-determined encoding on reparent** ✅ *provisional*: a reparented node's encoding
77	   is a pure function of its new surroundings — it takes the type of its nearest preceding
78	   sibling under the new parent (fallback: following sibling; no siblings: paragraph under a
79	   heading/root, list item under anything else). Consequences: paragraph → indent → outdent
80	   round-trips back to a paragraph (new sibling is a paragraph), while outdenting inside a
81	   100%-nested-list document keeps everything list items (all neighbors are list items) — no
82	   flattening, and no hidden per-node memory of original types.
83	4. **Mixed containment** ✅: a list item can never contain a heading — under the two-regime
84	   algebra no op can even attempt it (headings move by level, not by reparenting), so this
85	   holds by construction rather than by rejection.
86	5. **Leaf-only blocks** ✅: code fences, tables, callouts, quotes are movable/indentable
87	   **atoms**; internal content is not outline-structured.
88	6. **Toggle persistence** ✅: remembered per note in the **plugin data store** (consistent
89	   with Q3 — files stay clean; frontmatter would pollute content for a pure UI mode).
90	
91	See [05-org-mode-comparison.md](05-org-mode-comparison.md) for where this algebra aligns
92	with and diverges from org-mode, the closest living reference system.
93	
94	### Verdicts from the mapping-core implementation (2026-07-12, `mapping-core` change)
95	
96	Both provisional rules **held up** under property testing (byte-identity round-trip,
97	op-closure, inverse laws — thousands of generated cases). Findings to carry forward:
98	
99	- **Attachment rule: KEEP.** Confirmed consequence: "list item as the sibling directly
100	  after a paragraph" is unrepresentable — the tree generator itself had to fold such
101	  shapes into children, which is the rule working as designed. Scope refinement
102	  discovered: in v1 the rule applies at *section level* (root/heading children); inside
103	  a list item's children, a paragraph and a following list parse as siblings under the
104	  item. Revisit whether nested paragraphs should also capture lists.
105	- **Context-determined encoding: KEEP.** Delivered exactly the promised laws: paragraph
106	  indent∘outdent restores the document byte-identically, and pure-list documents never
107	  flatten. The donor scan considers only paragraph/list-item siblings (headings/atoms
108	  are skipped) — heading nodes are never produced by re-encoding.
109	- **New rejection discovered — outdent out of a heading section.** Heading scope is
110	  positional in markdown: content placed "after the section" is still *in* the section,
111	  so brother→uncle for a direct child of a heading has no encoding → rejected
112	  (`not-expressible-under-target`). UX implication for the CM6 layer: outdent at
113	  section level needs affordance messaging (or a future "split section" op).
114	- **Reordering across the heading/content divide is rejected**, and heading swaps
115	  require equal levels — same positional-encoding reason.
116	- **Minimal-edit tradeoff**: indenting a paragraph into an existing child list keeps
117	  the old separator blank line with the untouched sibling (a loose list — same tree).
118	  Cosmetic; a "tidy gaps" pass could be a later opt-in.
119	
120	## Q3. Node identity & metadata storage ✅ DECIDED
121	
122	Native `^block-id` **on demand** (only when a node is actually referenced); collapse state in
123	the **plugin data store**; files stay clean. Multiline nodes as standard markdown continuation
124	lines (to be specced in detail).
125	
126	## Q4. MVP cut ✅ DECIDED: small, solid core
127	
128	v1 = the enforced core editor (grammar + node selection + structure invariants) built on an
129	architecture that keeps every later layer open (fold persistence, zoom, structured backlinks,
130	refs/mirrors, drag-and-drop, search). Layers land one at a time on top of the stable core.
131	
132	**Refinement (second alignment)**: the core implements the **universal block-tree model from
133	day 1** — headings + paragraphs + lists as nodes, per Q2. The MVP is smaller in *features*
134	(minimal chrome, no zoom/backlinks/DnD yet), not in *model*. Retrofitting the tree model onto
135	a list-only core later would risk a rewrite.
136	
137	## Q5. Relationship to existing plugins ✅ DECIDED: build fresh
138	
139	Build fresh — Q2's universal mapping needs a tree-model core that the list-only engines don't
140	have. Use obsidian-outliner / zoom / pro-outliner as proof-of-primitive references; borrow
141	patterns with MIT attribution where genuinely useful. Coexistence: detect obsidian-outliner /
142	obsidian-zoom at load and show a one-time warning notice.
143	
144	## Q6. Interop & degradation guarantees ✅ CONFIRMED as hard requirements
145	
146	Files remain plain markdown, fully usable with the plugin disabled/uninstalled; no gratuitous
147	rewriting of untouched lines (whitespace, bullets, mtimes); other-tool edits (sync, mobile
148	without plugin, scripts) never corrupt anything on re-open. To be written into the project
149	spec as invariants.
150	
151	## Q7. Mobile support ✅ DECIDED: mobile-safe, desktop-tested
152	
153	Hard rules from day 1: no Node/Electron APIs, `isMobile`-aware, CM6/DOM only (the editor-
154	centric architecture already guarantees this). Test/polish desktop only for v1.0; declare
155	mobile support in a v1.x milestone after real-device testing.
156	
157	## Q8. Vim mode ✅ DECIDED: out of scope for v1
158	
159	The only known path is the undocumented `window.CodeMirrorAdapter.Vim` hack, which fails the
160	perfect-scorecard bar. Document as a known limitation.
161	
162	## Q9. Node content richness ✅ DECIDED: plain content in v1
163	
164	Checkboxes/tasks are plain content in v1 — preserved perfectly (isomorphism guarantees it),
165	no special node state or UX. First-class task states are a clean later layer.
166	
167	## Q10. Backlinks pane placement ❓ (post-v1)
168	
169	Our structured backlinks as: a sidebar pane (like core backlinks), an in-document footer
170	section (like influx/Logseq), or both? Replace-core-pane is off the table (private APIs).
171	
172	## Q11. Undo/redo scope ✅ DECIDED: CM6-native undo for v1
173	
174	With deliberate transaction grouping (`userEvent` annotations) so one structural op = one undo
175	step. View-state restoration (fold/zoom/focus) is a later enhancement layer.
176	
177	## Q12. Name & positioning ✅ DECIDED: working name stays; final name at submission
178	
179	"True Outliner" as working name; decide the final name at directory-submission time.
180	Differentiator statement: *any note is an outline — enforced structure, node selection,
181	isomorphic markdown mapping — one coherent plugin*.
182

input.prompt:
I need research (not code changes) to inform a decision about whether a markdown-outliner Obsidian plugin should replace its hand-rolled block-level markdown parser with a remark/mdast/micromark-based solution, or keep the custom parser. Use WebSearch and WebFetch. It's July 2026 — search with that timeframe in mind, don't assume 2024/2025 is current.

Answer these questions with sources:

1. **What parser does Obsidian core actually use today (2026), for both editing (CM6/Live Preview) and rendering?** There's a claim from a 2024-era forum post that "Obsidian uses remark-parse 8 or earlier with commonmark option enabled." Verify/update this: is Obsidian still on remark/mdast internally, or has it moved to something else (e.g. its own tokenizer, markdown-it, micromark)? Check the obsidian-api GitHub repo/typings, Obsidian's own changelog/help docs, forum.obsidian.md threads, and any dev-facing announcements. Note whether this is exposed to plugins via any public API (I already know `MarkdownRenderer.render()` exists but doesn't expose the parsed AST) — confirm there is still no public API to obtain a parsed syntax tree for a note.

2. **The mdast/remark/micromark ecosystem for Obsidian Flavored Markdown extensions.** OFM adds: wikilinks `[[note]]`/`[[note#heading]]`/`[[note|alias]]`, embeds `![[note]]`, block references `^block-id`, callouts `> [!note]`, tags `#tag`, highlights `==text==`, comments `%%text%%`, footnotes, math via `$...$`/`$$...$$`, checkboxes/tasks. For each: does a maintained micromark/remark extension exist (e.g. remark-wiki-link, micromark-extension-*, obsidian-specific mdast plugins)? Note maintenance status (last updated, npm downloads) — many of these are community, possibly abandoned. Also check if `remark-gfm` covers tables/strikethrough/tasklists adequately for OFM's GFM subset.

3. **Fidelity/round-trip behavior**: Does `mdast-util-to-markdown` (remark-stringify) preserve exact original formatting (whitespace, list marker style, blank-line patterns, indentation) on a parse→stringify round-trip, or does it normalize/reformat? Look for known issues/discussions about remark not being byte-preserving. Also check whether `mdast` position info (unist `Point`/`Position` with offsets) is reliable enough to slice the *original source string* for surgical edits instead of using the stringifier at all.

4. **micromark specifically** (the lower-level tokenizer remark/mdast is built on): can it be used standalone as just a CommonMark-compliant block-boundary/tokenizer oracle (event stream with offsets) without going through full mdast/remark stringification? Is there prior art of projects using micromark this way (as a "boundary detector" while keeping their own tree/serialization)?

Keep the final report well-organized under these 4 headings, cite URLs inline, and flag anything where you're not confident vs. clearly sourced. Don't recommend a decision — just gather the facts, I'll do the analysis. Aim for a thorough but not bloated report (roughly 600-900 words plus source links).

1	## Context
2	
3	First code in the repo. Everything later (CM6 enforcement, grammar, selection, zoom) consumes
4	this library, so its API shape and guarantees are load-bearing. The governing decisions live
5	in docs/research/04-open-questions.md (Q2 two-regime algebra, Q6 interop invariants) and
6	docs/research/05-org-mode-comparison.md. Two rules are explicitly provisional and this change
7	is their pressure test: list-after-paragraph attachment and context-determined encoding.
8	
9	## Goals / Non-Goals
10	
11	**Goals:**
12	
13	- A pure TypeScript library (zero Obsidian/CodeMirror imports) implementing parse, encode,
14	  and structural ops with machine-checked guarantees:
15	  1. **Byte-identity round-trip**: `encode(parse(md)) === md` for any input.
16	  2. **Op closure**: every accepted op yields a tree whose encoding re-parses to that tree.
17	  3. **Minimal edits**: an op's text output differs from the input only on lines the op had
18	     to touch (Q6: no gratuitous rewrites).
19	- An ops API that returns both the new tree and a minimal line-edit list, so the later CM6
20	  layer can dispatch real editor transactions instead of whole-document replaces.
21	- Findings on the provisional rules fed back into the decision log.
22	
23	**Non-Goals:**
24	
25	- No CM6/Obsidian integration, UI, keymaps, or persistence (later changes).
26	- No inline-level parsing (links, emphasis) — block structure only; node text is opaque.
27	- No full CommonMark conformance — we target Obsidian's block dialect, not the spec.
28	
29	## Decisions
30	
31	### D1. Hand-rolled line-based block parser (not remark/markdown-it/lezer)
32	
33	Byte-identity kills off-the-shelf parsers: mdast/markdown-it normalize on stringify, and
34	reconstructing text from their ASTs is exactly the lossy path Logseq fell into. We only need
35	*block-level* structure (headings, paragraphs, list items, fenced code, tables, quotes,
36	callouts, frontmatter, HTML blocks, thematic breaks), which is a line classification + stack
37	problem — small enough to own, and it must match *Obsidian's* dialect (callouts, task
38	markers) rather than strict CommonMark anyway. Lezer/`@codemirror/language` stays an option
39	for the CM6 layer later; the library's tree is the contract, not the parse technique.
40	
41	### D2. Nodes own verbatim line spans; encode = concatenation
42	
43	A node stores its original lines verbatim (marker, indentation, trailing whitespace and all);
44	`encode` concatenates spans. Round-trip identity is then structural rather than aspirational:
45	parse never interprets-and-reprints, it only *segments*. Ops that change a node produce new
46	lines for that node only. Blank lines are owned deterministically: a blank-line run belongs
47	to the gap after the preceding node (stored as its `trailingGap`), so segmentation is total
48	and unambiguous.
49	
50	### D3. Ops are pure functions returning `Result<{tree, edits}, Rejection>`
51	
52	`indent(tree, nodeId)`, `outdent(tree, nodeId)`, `moveUp/moveDown(tree, nodeId)`.
53	Rejections are typed values (`at-h6-bound`, `no-previous-sibling`, `at-top-level`, …), never
54	exceptions — the CM6 layer will map them to gentle UI feedback. `edits` is a list of
55	line-range replacements computed from changed nodes, enabling both the minimal-edit property
56	test and future CM6 transaction dispatch.
57	
58	### D4. The algebra implements the decision log verbatim
59	
60	- Headings: level ± 1, whole subtree shifts, reject at h1/h6 bounds; skips preserved;
61	  tree depth derives from levels.
62	- Other nodes: reparent; indent = child of previous sibling, outdent = brother→uncle;
63	  encoding of the landed node recomputed from nearest-preceding-sibling type (fallback
64	  following sibling; else paragraph under heading/root, list item elsewhere).
65	- List-after-paragraph = children of that paragraph; column-0 paragraph closes the group.
66	- Leaf blocks (code, table, callout, quote, HTML, thematic break) are atoms; frontmatter is
67	  an inert document preamble, not a node.
68	
69	Both provisional rules are isolated behind small strategy functions (`attachmentRule`,
70	`encodingRule`) so a future config option or rule revision is a local change.
71	
72	### D5. Testing: fast-check property tests + real-corpus fixtures, vitest runner
73	
74	Generators produce arbitrary valid trees (and adversarial markdown strings); properties are
75	the three guarantees in Goals plus op-specific laws (heading indent∘outdent = id away from
76	bounds; paragraph indent∘outdent = id per context-encoding). A `tests/corpus/` of real
77	markdown files (flat notes, deep lists, mixed docs, edge-case zoo) locks byte-identity
78	against reality, not just our generator's imagination.
79	
80	### D6. Tooling
81	
82	TypeScript strict, vitest, fast-check, eslint (+ `obsidianmd/eslint-plugin` configured now so
83	later layers inherit a clean scorecard baseline), esbuild deferred until the plugin change.
84	Plain npm, no monorepo — one package, `src/` + `tests/`.
85	
86	## Risks / Trade-offs
87	
88	- [Dialect drift: our parser disagrees with Obsidian's on some construct] → corpus tests
89	  include Obsidian-flavored fixtures (callouts, task lists, embeds); later CM6 change adds a
90	  cross-check against `CachedMetadata.sections` on real vaults; disagreements become fixtures.
91	- [Blank-line/indentation edge cases break byte-identity] → D2 makes identity structural;
92	  fuzzing with adversarial whitespace generators; any failure is a red build, not a latent bug.
93	- [Provisional rules prove wrong under testing] → they're strategy-isolated (D4); revising
94	  them is a rule swap + regenerated fixtures, and the decision log gets updated.
95	- [Ordered lists: moving items breaks numbering] → v1 policy: renumber only the lines the op
96	  already touches plus affected following siblings' markers — a deliberate, documented
97	  exception to "minimal edits" (markers only, never content). Property test encodes this.
98	- [Setext headings (`===`/`---` underlines) have no form at levels 3+] → parse them as h1/h2
99	  nodes; a level op that needs an inexpressible setext level rewrites that heading to ATX as
100	  part of the op (minimal, lossless, only-when-touched).
101	
102	## Open Questions
103	
104	*(resolved during implementation)*
105	
106	- Indentation unit: **2 spaces for newly created nesting**; existing indentation
107	  (including tabs) is preserved via relative shifts, so tab-indented vaults are not
108	  rewritten. Infer-from-file remains a candidate refinement for the CM6 change, where
109	  Obsidian's own indent settings are available.
110	- Heading `moveUp/moveDown`: **sibling-swap only, equal levels required** — swapping a
111	  heading with a non-heading sibling or a different-level heading has no positional
112	  encoding (the moved node would be re-scoped on reparse) and is rejected with
113	  `cannot-reorder-across-heading-boundary`.
114	- New discovery (fed back to the decision log): **outdent out of a heading section is
115	  rejected** — heading scope is positional, so a direct child of a heading has no
116	  "sibling of my section" spot. The CM6 layer needs UX for this.

<result># Research Report: remark/mdast/micromark vs. Custom Parser for Obsidian Outliner Plugin

## 1. What parser does Obsidian actually use (2026)?

**No confirmed answer — Obsidian's internal parser is closed-source and undocumented, and this hasn't changed.** There is no official spec or source-visible implementation.

- Obsidian dev team confirmed in 2021/2022 there's no reference spec: "There is no reference spec and is not currently on the roadmap" (WhiteNoise, Obsidian staff, Oct 2022), and "we try to use commonmark with some minor additions" (WhiteNoise, Dec 2021) — [Is there a parser/renderer reference spec?](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/is-there-a-parser-renderer-reference-spec/29504)
- The "remark-parse 8 or earlier with commonmark option" claim is **not from 2024** — it's from a forum post dated **April 11, 2026** (user ile-24556), explicitly labeled speculation based on observed edge-case similarities to Prettier/remark-parse-8, "unconfirmed by Obsidian developers" — [Obsidian Markdown Parser](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-markdown-parser/55922). A separate 2026-01-26 comment on the same thread calls the parser a "black box," complaining devs are "forced to add remark as a dependency" themselves rather than being given access to Obsidian's own parser.
- For the **editor (Live Preview/CM6)**: Obsidian's editor runs on CodeMirror 6, whose stock markdown mode uses the Lezer parser (`@lezer/markdown`, via `@codemirror/lang-markdown`). However, the community project `lezer-markdown-obsidian` (last pushed 2023-12-27) states explicitly: **"This is not the parser that Obsidian itself uses, and all parsing is a best attempt to match the way Obsidian parses markdown"** — [erykwalder/lezer-markdown-obsidian](https://github.com/erykwalder/lezer-markdown-obsidian). This strongly implies Obsidian ships a **proprietary/customized tokenizer** for Live Preview rather than the stock open-source Lezer markdown grammar.
- For **reading-mode rendering**: no verifiable public statement either way; community analyses variably guess remark-based or markdown-it-influenced behavior (e.g., one third-party doc attributes an HTML-block-parsing quirk to "the underlying CommonMark specification and the Markdown-it parser," but this is inference, not an Obsidian source statement).
- **No public API for a parsed AST exists, confirmed as still true.** `MarkdownRenderer.render()` produces HTML only, with `renderMarkdown` marked deprecated in typings ([obsidian-api](https://github.com/obsidianmd/obsidian-api), [docs.obsidian.md/MarkdownRenderer](https://docs.obsidian.md/Reference/TypeScript+API/MarkdownRenderer)). Forum thread ["Access parsed Markdown"](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/access-parsed-markdown/25398) (2021, no Obsidian staff reply) shows the standard workaround is plugin authors running their own `mdast-util-from-markdown` pass on the raw file text. This pattern is unchanged in 2026 per the newer forum thread above.

**Confidence: high** that no public AST API exists and the internal engine is undocumented; **low-to-moderate** on any specific claim about which library (if any pre-existing one) Obsidian's core is actually built on — treat all such claims, including the remark-8 one, as community speculation.

## 2. OFM extensions in the remark/micromark ecosystem

Mixed maturity; most are small single-maintainer community packages, several stale.

| Feature | Package(s) | Status (GitHub `pushed_at`, stars) |
|---|---|---|
| Wikilinks `[[note]]`/`#heading`/`\|alias` | `remark-wiki-link` (landakram) | **Stale**: last push 2023-10-10, 109★, marked "Inactive" by Snyk/libraries.io |
| Wikilinks (forks) | `@portaljs/remark-wiki-link`, `remark-wiki-link-plus` | Active forks exist but are third-party maintained forks of the above, not upstream |
| Combined OFM (wikilinks+embeds+callouts+highlights) | `remark-obsidian-md` (per libraries.io v1.1.0) | Not independently verified for freshness — could not fetch npm page (403) |
| Combined OFM (alt) | `johackim/remark-obsidian` | Active-ish: pushed 2026-05-10, 69★ |
| Combined OFM (alt) | `heavycircle/remark-obsidian` | pushed 2025-07-15, 9★ (smaller) |
| Combined OFM (alt, archived) | `alfredoperez/remark-obsidian` | **Archived**, pushed 2022-05-20, 0★ |
| Callouts `> [!note]` | `r4ai/remark-callout` (also `@r4ai/remark-callout`) | Most active found: pushed **2026-07-11**, 44★, 15 open issues |
| Callouts (alt) | `escwxyz/remark-obsidian-callout` | **Archived**, last push 2025-09-03 |
| Callouts (non-remark alt) | `markdown-it-obsidian-callouts`, `goldmark-obsidian-callout` (Go) | Exist for other ecosystems, confirming callouts are a recurring "everyone reimplements this" pain point |
| Highlights `==text==` | `remark-flexible-markers` (ipikuka) | Active: pushed 2026-05-25, 22★ — general-purpose `<mark>` plugin, not Obsidian-specific but covers the syntax |
| Comments `%%text%%` | Bundled inside combined `remark-obsidian*` packages only; no dedicated standalone package found |
| Block references `^block-id` | **No dedicated remark/micromark plugin found** in searches; combined `remark-obsidian` packages may include partial support but this wasn't independently confirmed — flagged as low confidence |
| Tags `#tag` | Not separately searched but is a simple regex-level feature; likely bundled in combined packages or trivial to hand-roll |
| Footnotes | Covered by `remark-gfm` (footnotes are part of its scope) |
| Math `$...$` / `$$...$$` | `remark-math` — actively maintained, v6.0.0, GitHub pushed 2025-02-20, 512★, Node 16+ compatible |
| Embeds `![[note]]` | Bundled in combined `remark-obsidian*` packages; no standalone plugin found |
| Tables/strikethrough/tasklists (GFM subset) | `remark-gfm` — well-maintained: v4.0.1, GitHub pushed 2025-02-10, **1,224★**, 28.5M weekly downloads, "sustainable" maintenance rating. Covers tables, strikethrough, tasklists, autolink literals, and footnotes together, which is a good match for OFM's GFM subset. |

**Overall picture:** the foundational/general packages (`remark-gfm`, `remark-math`, `mdast-util-*`, `micromark` core) are solid and actively maintained by the unified/syntax-tree/micromark core teams. The **Obsidian-specific extension layer is fragmented across many small, competing, single-maintainer packages** with inconsistent feature coverage (wikilinks, callouts, comments, block-refs, embeds) — you would likely need to hand-write or heavily fork at least callouts, comments, and block-references rather than adopt a package wholesale. Flag: I could not verify `remark-obsidian-md`'s actual freshness/feature completeness directly (npm 403'd my fetch) — worth checking manually before relying on it.

## 3. Round-trip / stringify fidelity

**mdast-util-to-markdown (remark-stringify) is explicitly not byte-preserving and not fully structure-preserving.** This is a known, acknowledged limitation, not a niche bug:

- Confirmed open issue: [`mdast-util-to-markdown` #12](https://github.com/syntax-tree/mdast-util-to-markdown/issues/12) — parsing `***emphasis*in emphasis*` and stringifying it back produces `\***emphasis*in emphasis*`, which **re-parses into a structurally different AST** (nested emphasis collapses to flat). Labeled "confirmed," still open as of my check (repo last pushed 2026-04-24, 8 open issues total), no maintainer resolution stating round-tripping is a design goal.
- General project stance (from repo docs/README and community discussion threads like [remark-stringify formatting questions #1166](https://github.com/orgs/remarkjs/discussions/1166)): the stringifier "does its best to serialize markdown to match the syntax tree," but complete round-tripping is stated to be impossible in general since arbitrary values can be injected into the tree, and there are known issues around escaping and spacing preferences that aren't fully configurable.
- **Practical implication**: for a tool that must preserve exact source formatting (whitespace, list marker style `-` vs `*`, blank-line patterns, indentation) across incremental edits, remark-stringify alone is not safe to trust for un-touched parts of the document.

**On using position offsets to slice the original source instead of stringifying:** unist `Position`/`Point` nodes do carry 0-indexed `offset` fields alongside line/column, and `mdast-util-from-markdown` populates these reliably for CommonMark-compliant parses (I did not find any specific report of the offsets being unreliable or missing for standard nodes). This is a well-established pattern (also used by tools like Prettier's markdown printer and various codemods) — slice the raw source with node offsets for content you didn't change, and only synthesize new markdown for nodes you actually created/modified. I did not find a single dedicated package/utility explicitly branded for this "surgical slice" pattern, but the primitives (`unist-util-position`, position offsets themselves) support it. **Confidence: moderate** — this is inferred from unist's documented data model plus known practice in the ecosystem (e.g., Prettier), not from an explicit case study I found and verified.

## 4. micromark as a standalone tokenizer/boundary detector

**Yes, this usage pattern is supported by design and has prior art**, though I found less explicit "here's a project doing exactly that" documentation than expected.

- micromark is explicitly architected as a layered system: the core tokenizer (`micromark-core-commonmark` + `micromark`) emits a flat event stream (start/end events referencing token spans with position/offset info) that is **decoupled from any specific output**. The micromark README states the parsing layer "can still be used separately to build ASTs, CSTs, or many other output formats" beyond its own HTML compiler — [micromark/micromark](https://github.com/micromark/micromark) (repo pushed 2025-05-10, 2,196★, actively maintained, 15 open issues).
- `mdast-util-from-markdown` itself is the reference example of consuming micromark's event stream and building a different tree shape (mdast) on top — i.e., "use micromark for tokenization, build your own tree" is literally how remark itself is built, which is the strongest piece of prior art: the ecosystem's own two-layer design (micromark = tokenizer, mdast-util-from-markdown = tree builder) is exactly the "boundary detector + keep your own tree" pattern you're asking about.
- I did not find a clearly documented third-party project that uses bare micromark (without any `mdast-util-*` tree builder) purely as a boundary/offset oracle while feeding a fully custom, non-mdast data structure. This may well exist (e.g., inside MDX tooling, Prettier's markdown plugin, or Astro's content pipeline) but I could not confirm it directly — **flag as unverified**, worth a targeted look at Prettier's `markdown` printer source or Astro's content-collections markdown handling if this pattern matters to the decision.

Here's the synthesis. Short version: **the concern is legitimate but the current decision (custom parser, [design.md](openspec/changes/archive/2026-07-12-mapping-core/design.md) D1) holds up** — full remark/mdast adoption would actually *reintroduce* the exact risk you're trying to avoid. There's one narrower option worth keeping on the radar, not adopting now.

## What the research changed vs. the original framing

**The "Obsidian uses remark-parse 8" claim doesn't hold up.** It traces to an April 2026 forum post that's explicitly labeled speculation, on a thread where Obsidian's own devs have said there's no reference spec and no plans for one ("we try to use commonmark with some minor additions" is the closest thing to an official statement). The Live Preview editor's community-maintained Lezer grammar (`lezer-markdown-obsidian`) states outright it is *not* what Obsidian uses internally — it's a best-effort approximation. So there's no confirmed off-the-shelf library backing Obsidian core, and — more importantly — no public API exposes whatever AST it does build. This was already your working assumption; the research just confirms it more firmly. Practically it means: adopting remark wouldn't get you closer to "matching Obsidian's actual parsing," because nobody outside Obsidian knows what that is. You'd still be reverse-engineering the dialect from behavior and corpus fixtures either way — which is exactly what you're already doing.

## The byte-identity blocker is real, not hypothetical

`mdast-util-to-markdown` (remark-stringify) is **documented as non-round-tripping**, with an open, confirmed upstream issue where parse→stringify changes AST structure on re-parse for a case as simple as nested emphasis. The remark team's own position is that full round-tripping isn't a design goal. That's a direct collision with your Goal 1 (`encode(parse(md)) === md`) and Goal 3 (minimal edits — untouched lines must not move). Adopting remark/mdast wholesale would mean either accepting silent reformatting of a user's file (a hard no per your Q6 interop invariants) or building your own slice-from-offsets layer on top anyway to avoid ever calling the stringifier — at which point you've kept remark's parser but thrown away its main value proposition.

## The OFM extension layer is not the mature ecosystem it might look like

Wikilinks, callouts, embeds, block-refs, and comments each have community remark plugins, but they're fragmented across several competing single-maintainer packages, and several are stale or archived (`remark-wiki-link` inactive since 2023, multiple `remark-obsidian` forks abandoned). Only `remark-gfm` and `remark-math` are genuinely solid, widely-used dependencies. You'd likely end up hand-writing or forking the Obsidian-specific bits regardless — and since your model already treats all of these (wikilinks, callouts, `^block-id`, tags, `$$math$$`, `%%comments%%`) as **opaque content inside block-level nodes** rather than parsing them structurally (confirmed by [04-obsidian-flavored.md](tests/corpus/04-obsidian-flavored.md)), you don't actually need inline-level parsing for any of this. That's a scope mismatch with what remark/mdast is built to do (full inline AST) versus what your architecture needs (block segmentation only, content stays verbatim).

## The one option worth banking, not building

There is legitimate architecture for using **micromark's core tokenizer alone** (not mdast, not remark) purely as a CommonMark-correct block-boundary oracle: it emits a flat event stream with real offsets into the source, decoupled from any tree shape — this is literally how `mdast-util-from-markdown` itself is built on top of micromark, so the "tokenize with micromark, build your own tree" pattern is proven, just not by a third party doing exactly your thing. If your hand-rolled segmenter's CommonMark edge cases (lazy continuation, blockquote/list nesting corner cases — several already called out as deliberate simplifications in [parse.ts](src/parse.ts:6)) start causing real corpus failures against actual vaults, that's the upgrade path: keep `OutlineNode`/`encode`/ops exactly as they are, swap only the line-classification stage to consume micromark's event offsets instead of regexes. It's a contained, testable substitution (diff old vs. new segmenter over the corpus) — not a reason to touch the architecture today.

**Recommendation:** don't switch. Revisit only if/when your own corpus testing surfaces dialect-correctness bugs that a hand-rolled segmenter is structurally bad at — and even then, reach for micromark-as-tokenizer, not full remark/mdast.

1	# Research: A True Outliner for Obsidian
2	
3	Initial research for the **obsidian-true-outliner** project — an Obsidian plugin that turns
4	the editor into a true outliner experience (Logseq / Workflowy / Roam-class), built cleanly
5	on public plugin APIs.
6	
7	Research date: July 2026.
8	
9	## Documents
10	
11	| Doc | Contents |
12	| --- | --- |
13	| [01-outliner-landscape.md](01-outliner-landscape.md) | What a "true outliner" is: the reference apps, the catalog of defining behaviors, lessons (good and bad) from each app |
14	| [02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md](02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md) | Existing Obsidian plugins in this space, how they work, and the gap analysis |
15	| [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md) | Can the experience be built on public APIs only? Architecture options, guidelines/scorecard constraints, verdict |
16	| [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md) | Decisions that need alignment before any planning/spec work |
17	| [05-org-mode-comparison.md](05-org-mode-comparison.md) | Where our mapping algebra aligns with / diverges from org-mode, the closest living reference system |
18	
19	## TL;DR
20	
21	- A **true outliner** is defined by one invariant: *the document is a tree of nodes, and every
22	  operation — typing, selecting, deleting, moving, pasting — respects node boundaries*. Existing
23	  Obsidian plugins (obsidian-outliner et al.) bolt keyboard tricks onto flat text; the invariant
24	  is never enforced, so the structure is always one careless selection away from breaking.
25	- The reference apps split on one architectural axis: **markdown files as source of truth**
26	  (Logseq OG, outl) vs **database as source of truth** (Roam, Tana, Orca, new Logseq).
27	  Logseq's multi-year rewrite and its 2026 split into two products is the cautionary tale for
28	  the file-based approach done with too much in-file metadata; **outl's sidecar-CRDT design**
29	  is the cleanest known answer (markdown stays clean, IDs live out-of-band).
30	- Obsidian gives us more native building blocks than expected: the **metadata cache already
31	  parses list hierarchy** (`ListItemCache` with parent links) for every file in the vault,
32	  **block IDs (`^abc123`), block links and block embeds are native**, folding is native, and
33	  the whole CodeMirror 6 extension surface is an official, documented API
34	  (`registerEditorExtension`).
35	- **Feasibility verdict: yes, with one architectural fork in the road.** An *editor-centric*
36	  approach (CM6 extensions inside the standard markdown view) can deliver the large majority of
37	  the target UX with 100% public APIs — proven piecemeal by obsidian-outliner, obsidian-zoom,
38	  and obsidian-pro-outliner (which already ships zoom + breadcrumbs, Tana-style mirrors, and
39	  Workflowy-style selection expansion on public APIs). A *custom-view* approach (Kanban-style)
40	  gives total UX control but today requires monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState`
41	  (private internals) or abandoning `.md` (losing links/backlinks/graph) — both at odds with
42	  the "perfect scorecard, no hacks" goal.
43	- Obsidian's 2026 **Community directory with automated safety scorecards** (plus the official
44	  `obsidianmd/eslint-plugin`) makes "perfect scorecard" a concrete, checkable target rather
45	  than an aspiration.
46	
47	## Decisions so far (2026-07-12, two alignment rounds)
48	
49	1. **Architecture**: editor-centric — CM6 extensions in the standard markdown view + own side
50	   panes; 100% public API.
51	2. **Scope**: the **universal isomorphic outline view** — any markdown note maps onto a block
52	   tree (headings / paragraphs / lists / other blocks as nodes) and can be toggled into the
53	   outliner editing experience and back, losslessly. Not a list-only mode, not a vault takeover.
54	3. **Metadata**: native `^block-id` only on demand; collapse state in plugin data; clean files.
55	4. **v1**: small solid core (grammar + node selection + enforced invariants) implementing the
56	   **universal tree model from day 1** — smaller in features, not in model; fold persistence,
57	   zoom, structured backlinks, refs/mirrors as later layers.
58	5. **Mapping algebra**: every structural op writes the minimal markdown encoding of the new
59	   tree, or is rejected with gentle feedback when no encoding exists — never hidden state,
60	   never lossy conversion. Two regimes: **headings** = org-mode promote/demote (Tab/S-Tab is
61	   level ± 1, subtree shifts, tree re-derives from levels, reject only at h1/h6 bounds);
62	   **everything else** = reparent (child-of-previous-sibling / brother→uncle) with encoding
63	   recomputed from the new context (nearest-sibling type — so paragraphs round-trip and
64	   nested-list docs never flatten). A list after a paragraph is that paragraph's children
65	   (provisional); leaf blocks are atoms. Org-mode alignment/divergence:
66	   [05-org-mode-comparison.md](05-org-mode-comparison.md).
67	6. **Everything else**: build fresh (existing plugins as references only); interop/degradation
68	   guarantees are hard invariants; mobile-safe from day 1, desktop-tested for v1.0; vim out of
69	   scope v1; tasks are plain content v1; CM6-native undo v1; toggle state in plugin data.
70	
71	All pre-planning questions are decided (Q10 backlinks-placement deferred as post-v1):
72	[04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md).
73	
74	## Where this project starts from
75	
76	- `../../openspec/` — OpenSpec (spec-driven development) scaffold, already initialized.
77	- `~/Code/tui-outliner-multiline` — the author's TUI outliner prototype (SpecKit-based).
78	  Its specs are a useful inspiration for behavior inventories (navigation, structural ops,
79	  zoom focus rules, undo/redo focus restoration, multiline nodes), with the caveat that its
80	  modal (vim-like nav/edit split) interaction model is TUI-specific and does **not** transfer
81	  to Obsidian, which is a direct-manipulation, always-editing environment.

## Q13. Parser: custom vs. remark/mdast/micromark ✅ DECIDED (2026-07-13): keep custom, revisit trigger defined

Re-examined design.md D1 against 2026 research before committing to the hand-rolled parser
long-term. **Verdict: keep it.** Findings:

- Obsidian's internal parser is an undocumented black box; the "remark-parse 8" claim
  circulating in the forums is unverified speculation, not a confirmed fact — there's nothing
  to "align with" even if we wanted to.
- `mdast-util-to-markdown` is documented as **not** round-trip-safe (confirmed upstream issue:
  parse→stringify can change AST structure on re-parse). Adopting it would directly break the
  byte-identity goal (design.md D1/D2) — the exact failure mode D1 already rejected it for.
- The OFM-extension remark plugin ecosystem (wikilinks, callouts, embeds, block-refs) is
  fragmented and often stale/single-maintainer; we'd hand-write most of it anyway — and we
  don't need it, since our model keeps all OFM constructs as opaque content inside block
  nodes (never inline-parsed).
- **Revisit trigger**: if corpus/property testing surfaces real CommonMark-dialect bugs our
  segmenter is structurally bad at (lazy continuation, nested list/blockquote edge cases),
  the upgrade path is **micromark's core tokenizer only** (not mdast/remark) as a
  boundary/offset oracle feeding our existing `OutlineNode`/encode/ops unchanged — this
  "tokenize with micromark, keep your own tree" pattern is exactly how `mdast-util-from-markdown`
  itself is built, so it's proven architecture, just not something to adopt preemptively.

1	# structural-operations Specification
2	
3	## Purpose
4	TBD - created by archiving change mapping-core. Update Purpose after archive.
5	## Requirements
6	### Requirement: Operation results are total and typed
7	Every structural operation (indent, outdent, moveUp, moveDown) SHALL be a pure function
8	returning either an accepted result — the new tree plus a minimal list of line-range edits —
9	or a typed rejection (e.g. `at-h6-bound`, `no-previous-sibling`, `at-top-level`). Operations
10	SHALL never throw for algebra reasons and a rejection SHALL leave the document unchanged.
11	
12	#### Scenario: Rejection is a value, not an exception
13	- **WHEN** an operation cannot be expressed in markdown (e.g. indenting an h6 heading)
14	- **THEN** the function returns a typed rejection identifying the reason, and the tree and
15	  document text are unchanged
16	
17	### Requirement: Heading indent and outdent shift levels
18	Indent on a heading SHALL increase its level by one and outdent SHALL decrease it by one,
19	rewriting the heading markers of the node and its entire heading subtree (level shift is
20	recursive), touching only heading-marker characters. The tree SHALL re-derive from the new
21	levels. Indent SHALL be rejected at h6; outdent SHALL be rejected at h1.
22	
23	#### Scenario: Demote with subtree
24	- **WHEN** indent is applied to `## Budget` which contains `### Transport`
25	- **THEN** the document now reads `### Budget` and `#### Transport`, all non-heading lines
26	  are byte-identical, and `Budget` re-parses as a child of the preceding `##` heading
27	
28	#### Scenario: Outdent consumes a level skip before changing hierarchy
29	- **WHEN** outdent is applied to `### Monday` whose parent is `# Log`
30	- **THEN** it becomes `## Monday`, still a child of `# Log` (level normalized, hierarchy
31	  unchanged), and a second outdent produces `# Monday` as a sibling of `# Log`
32	
33	#### Scenario: Demote may create a skip
34	- **WHEN** indent is applied to `### Electronics` whose parent is `## Packing` and which has
35	  no `###` sibling context requiring otherwise
36	- **THEN** it becomes `#### Electronics`, remaining a child of `## Packing` (a styling-only
37	  edit; tree position unchanged)
38	
39	#### Scenario: Bound rejections
40	- **WHEN** indent is applied to an h6 heading, or outdent to an h1 heading
41	- **THEN** the operation is rejected with `at-h6-bound` / `at-h1-bound` respectively
42	
43	### Requirement: Non-heading indent reparents under previous sibling
44	Indent on a non-heading node SHALL make it the last child of its previous sibling, and SHALL
45	be rejected with `no-previous-sibling` when none exists. The node's subtree moves with it.
46	
47	#### Scenario: Paragraph indented under paragraph
48	- **WHEN** indent is applied to top-level paragraph `Second thought.` whose previous sibling
49	  is paragraph `First thought.`
50	- **THEN** the document encodes `Second thought.` as a list item (`- Second thought.`)
51	  following the intact `First thought.` paragraph, and it re-parses as that paragraph's child
52	
53	#### Scenario: Indented node joins an existing child list
54	- **WHEN** indent is applied to paragraph `B.` whose previous sibling paragraph `A.` already
55	  has list-item children
56	- **THEN** `B.` becomes the last item of that existing list
57	
58	### Requirement: Non-heading outdent moves brother to uncle
59	Outdent on a non-heading node SHALL make it the next sibling of its former parent
60	(brother→uncle), subtree included, and SHALL be rejected with `at-top-level` when the node
61	has no parent to escape.
62	
63	#### Scenario: Outdent with children keeps the subtree attached
64	- **WHEN** outdent is applied to list item `x` (child of paragraph `Para.`) where `x` has
65	  child `y`
66	- **THEN** `x` becomes `Para.`'s next sibling with `y` still its child, expressed via the
67	  attachment rule
68	
69	### Requirement: Context-determined encoding on reparent (provisional rule)
70	A reparented non-heading node's markdown encoding SHALL be recomputed as a pure function of
71	its new surroundings: it takes the block type of its nearest preceding sibling under the new
72	parent; if none, the following sibling; if it has no siblings, it encodes as a paragraph
73	under a heading or the root, and as a list item under any other parent. This rule SHALL be
74	implemented behind an isolated strategy function.
75	
76	#### Scenario: Indent then outdent restores a paragraph
77	- **WHEN** a top-level paragraph is indented under a paragraph and then outdented back
78	- **THEN** it is re-encoded as a paragraph (nearest sibling at the destination is a
79	  paragraph) and the document is byte-identical to the original
80	
81	#### Scenario: Nested-list documents never flatten
82	- **WHEN** outdent is applied to any item in a document consisting entirely of nested list
83	  items
84	- **THEN** the item remains a list item at its new depth (all destination siblings are list
85	  items)
86	
87	### Requirement: Sibling reordering
88	MoveUp/moveDown SHALL swap a node (with its entire subtree) with its previous/next sibling,
89	and SHALL be rejected when no such sibling exists. Node types and encodings are unchanged by
90	reordering, except ordered-list markers which are renumbered.
91	
92	#### Scenario: Heading section swap
93	- **WHEN** moveUp is applied to `## Budget` preceded by sibling `## Packing`
94	- **THEN** the two sections (headings plus all descendant content) swap positions and every
95	  moved line is byte-identical to before, merely relocated
96	
97	### Requirement: Atoms move as opaque units
98	Structural operations on leaf atoms (code fences, tables, callouts, quotes, HTML blocks)
99	SHALL move or re-indent the whole block as one unit without ever treating its internal lines
100	as nodes.
101	
102	#### Scenario: Code fence indented under a list item
103	- **WHEN** indent is applied to a code fence whose previous sibling is a list item
104	- **THEN** every line of the fence is re-indented uniformly as list-item continuation
105	  content and the fence's internal text is otherwise unchanged
106	
107	### Requirement: Operation closure over the mapping
108	For every accepted operation, encoding the resulting tree SHALL produce valid markdown that
109	re-parses to an identical tree, and the emitted edit list applied to the original text SHALL
110	equal that encoding. Edits SHALL touch only lines the operation semantically requires, with
111	one documented exception: ordered-list marker renumbering of affected siblings.
112	
113	#### Scenario: Closure property test
114	- **WHEN** any generated operation is applied to any generated tree
115	- **THEN** either it is rejected, or `parse(encode(result.tree))` equals `result.tree` and
116	  applying `result.edits` to the source text yields `encode(result.tree)`
117

1	# document-tree-mapping Specification
2	
3	## Purpose
4	TBD - created by archiving change mapping-core. Update Purpose after archive.
5	## Requirements
6	### Requirement: Lossless round-trip
7	The library SHALL parse any markdown document into a block tree and encode any parsed tree
8	back to markdown such that `encode(parse(md))` is byte-identical to `md` — including
9	indentation style, list markers, trailing whitespace, and blank-line runs.
10	
11	#### Scenario: Arbitrary document round-trips byte-identically
12	- **WHEN** any markdown text (including adversarial whitespace, mixed indentation, and
13	  Obsidian-flavored constructs) is parsed and re-encoded without modification
14	- **THEN** the output is byte-identical to the input
15	
16	#### Scenario: Property test over generated and corpus documents
17	- **WHEN** the round-trip property runs over fast-check-generated documents and the
18	  real-world fixture corpus
19	- **THEN** no counterexample exists (a failure fails the build)
20	
21	### Requirement: Block node taxonomy
22	The parser SHALL segment a document into nodes of these types: heading, paragraph,
23	list item (bulleted, ordered, task), and leaf atoms (fenced code block, table, callout,
24	blockquote, HTML block, thematic break). YAML frontmatter SHALL be treated as an inert
25	document preamble, not a node. Atom internals SHALL NOT be parsed as nodes.
26	
27	#### Scenario: Mixed document segmentation
28	- **WHEN** a document containing frontmatter, headings, paragraphs, nested lists, a code
29	  fence, and a callout is parsed
30	- **THEN** each block becomes exactly one node of the corresponding type, the frontmatter is
31	  attached to the document root as preamble, and the code fence and callout are single atom
32	  nodes regardless of their internal line content
33	
34	### Requirement: Heading hierarchy derives from levels
35	Tree depth for headings SHALL be derived from heading levels: a heading is the child of the
36	nearest preceding heading of lower level (else the document root). Skipped levels (e.g. h1
37	followed by h3) SHALL be preserved verbatim, with the deeper heading as a direct child —
38	tree depth is tree position, not raw level. Content between a heading and the next heading
39	SHALL be children of that heading.
40	
41	#### Scenario: Skipped level preserved
42	- **WHEN** a document contains `# Log` followed by `### Monday`
43	- **THEN** `### Monday` parses as a direct child of `# Log` at tree depth 2, and re-encoding
44	  leaves the `###` marker unchanged
45	
46	### Requirement: List-after-paragraph attachment (provisional rule)
47	A list whose nearest preceding sibling block is a paragraph SHALL parse as the children of
48	that paragraph. A list directly following a heading (no paragraph between) SHALL parse as
49	direct children of the heading. A column-0 paragraph following a list SHALL be a sibling of
50	the preceding paragraph, closing the group. This rule SHALL be implemented behind an
51	isolated strategy function so it can be revised or made configurable.
52	
53	#### Scenario: List attaches to preceding paragraph
54	- **WHEN** a section contains paragraph `Clothes notes.` followed by list items `shirts`
55	  and `socks`, followed by column-0 paragraph `Another thought.`
56	- **THEN** `shirts` and `socks` parse as children of `Clothes notes.`, and
57	  `Another thought.` parses as the next sibling of `Clothes notes.`
58	
59	#### Scenario: List directly under a heading
60	- **WHEN** a heading is immediately followed by a list with no intervening paragraph
61	- **THEN** the list items parse as direct children of the heading
62	
63	### Requirement: Total, deterministic segmentation
64	Every line of the document SHALL belong to exactly one node span (or the preamble), with
65	blank-line runs owned as the trailing gap of the preceding node, so that encoding is pure
66	span concatenation.
67	
68	#### Scenario: Blank-line ownership
69	- **WHEN** two paragraphs are separated by three blank lines
70	- **THEN** the blank lines belong to the first paragraph's trailing gap and re-encode
71	  verbatim
72	
73	### Requirement: Minimal re-encoding after tree edits
74	When a tree is modified and re-encoded, all lines belonging to unmodified nodes SHALL be
75	byte-identical to the original; only nodes the modification touched may produce new lines.
76
